food_subgroup: Braised pork and offal assortment with mixed vegetables; Chinese lu wei (braised spiced) style platter

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for a mixed braised pork and offal assortment platter. FOODON_03315116 (pork dish) is the closest defensible parent. A more specific term for lu wei or braised offal platter does not exist in FoodOn as of 2026.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00GJ (pig meat and offal, cooked) is the closest defensible parent for this mixed braised pork and offal platter. No specific FoodEx2 code exists for a lu wei assortment platter with vegetables.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT food concept exists for a mixed braised pork and offal assortment platter. SNOMED CT food ontology does not cover composite Chinese lu wei dishes at this specificity.

preparation_method: slow-cooked in spiced soy-based master stock
served cold or at room temperature

serving.natural_language_portion: One shared platter of approximately 600 g, typically served as a starter or side dish for 3–5 people at a Chinese restaurant or zi char setting in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated composite from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy: Pork ear cooked (NDB 10193), Pork feet cooked (NDB 10040), Pork tongue cooked (NDB 10085), Pork knuckle (NDB 10038), Quail egg cooked (NDB 01140), and mixed vegetables (cucumber NDB 11206, green pepper NDB 11333, romaine lettuce NDB 11251, green onion NDB 11291, cherry tomato NDB 11529); weighted composite at approximately 70% pork/offal/egg and 30% vegetables by mass
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved source entry exists for this composite platter. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy individual ingredient entries, weighted at approximately 70% pork offal and egg components and 30% mixed vegetables by mass. Braising in soy-based stock adds sodium; sodium value is adjusted upward to reflect typical lu wei braising sodium contribution. Values represent a plausible per-100g composite for the assembled platter.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed meat and vegetable dishes estimated from analogous low-GI protein-dominant foods; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this composite braised pork and offal platter. GI is estimated at 25 (low) based on the dominant protein and fat composition of the dish (approximately 70% pork offal and egg by mass) with a minor vegetable carbohydrate contribution. Protein- and fat-dominant dishes consistently yield low GI values in the University of Sydney GI Database. Analogous reference: cooked pork dishes and egg dishes with GI values in the 20–35 range per Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (25 × 25.2) / 100 = 6.3, where carbohydrate per 600g serving = 4.2 × 6 = 25.2g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy-based braising stock
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its high sodium content (approximately 520 mg per 100g, equivalent to ~3120 mg sodium per 600g platter), which substantially exceeds the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg sodium; portion should be strictly restricted if consumed.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and low carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish has a low glycemic index (estimated 25) and low carbohydrate content (4.2g per 100g), making it a relatively suitable protein-rich option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; however, the high sodium content from braising stock warrants caution, and portion size should be moderated.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High cholesterol, saturated fat, and sodium from pork offal and braising stock
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should exercise caution with this dish due to its high cholesterol content (approximately 145 mg per 100g) from pork offal components, moderate saturated fat (2.8g per 100g), and high sodium (520 mg per 100g), all of which are established risk factors for adverse cardiovascular outcomes; consumption should be infrequent and portions small.

llm_training.natural_language_description: The Smoked Selection Platter (十全十美烟熏精选拼盘) is a Chinese-style braised and smoked assortment platter served at Chinese restaurants and zi char establishments in Singapore. The dish features a curated selection of braised pork offal and protein components — including pig ear, pork knuckle, pork trotters, pig tongue, and quail eggs — arranged alongside fresh and lightly dressed vegetables such as green pepper, romaine lettuce, cucumber, green onion, and cherry tomato. The pork and offal components are slow-cooked in a spiced soy-based master stock (lu wei), which imparts a deep savory, slightly sweet, and aromatic flavor profile with a firm yet gelatinous texture characteristic of well-braised collagen-rich cuts. The dish name 十全十美 (shí quán shí měi) is a Chinese idiom meaning 'perfect in every way' or 'ten out of ten', reflecting the celebratory and auspicious nature of the platter. Nutritionally, the dish is protein-dense (approximately 14g protein per 100g) with moderate fat (8.5g per 100g), significant cholesterol from offal components (approximately 145 mg per 100g), and high sodium (approximately 520 mg per 100g) from the braising stock. The glycemic index is low (estimated 25) due to the dominant protein and fat composition. Clinically, the dish is of concern for individuals with hypertension and cardiovascular disease due to its high sodium and cholesterol content, but is relatively suitable for individuals managing blood glucose due to its low carbohydrate and low GI profile. The platter is typically shared among 3–5 diners and is served as a starter or communal side dish. It is not halal due to pork content.

confidence_reason: No single approved source entry exists for this composite restaurant platter. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy individual ingredient entries weighted by approximate mass proportion (70% pork offal and egg, 30% vegetables). The GI is an analogous estimate based on protein- and fat-dominant dish characteristics from the University of Sydney GI Database reference tables. Confidence is medium rather than low because the individual ingredient sources are well-established USDA SR Legacy entries and the weighting rationale is defensible.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not consistently reported across the USDA SR Legacy entries used for this composite estimate and cannot be reliably derived for this mixed dish. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as selenium values are not reported for all pork offal components in the USDA SR Legacy entries used. Sodium value (520 mg per 100g) is adjusted upward from the raw USDA ingredient composite to reflect the substantial sodium contribution of soy-based lu wei braising stock, consistent with published sodium levels for Chinese braised pork dishes. The dish contains pork and is not halal or kosher. The platter is a shared dish; per-100g values represent the assembled mixed platter. GI estimated at 25 (low) based on analogous protein- and fat-dominant cooked pork and egg dishes per Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables; no direct GI measurement for this composite dish exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per serving = (25 × 25.2) / 100 = 6.3, where carbohydrate per 600g serving = 25.2g. The dish name 十全十美 is a Chinese idiom meaning 'perfect in every way'. The restaurant 'See Ya Tonight 今晚有局' is a Singapore-based Chinese restaurant chain.
